coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic insight into the enhanced photodegradation by black titanium dioxide nanofiber-graphene quantum dot composites
coredata.dc:creator: Shafiee, Ali
Carrier, Andrew J.
Nganou, Collins
Ehsan, Muhammad Fahad
Aibaghi, Behzad
Oakes, Ken D.
Zhang, Xu
coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic activity of black titanium dioxide nanofibers (bTiO2 NFs) can be enhanced through composition with carbon nanomaterials. However, it is unclear whether enhanced charge separation or increased surface adsorption of organic pollutants to the nanocomposite surfaces is the dominant contributor. This knowledge gap was filled when we sought to enhance the surface contact of carbon with the bTiO2 NFs by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hypothesizing that this would further enhance photocatalytic activity. The results showed a similar Rhodamine B (RhB) photodegradation rate between the optimal loadings of GQDs and RGO we reported previously. This allowed for further elucidation of the enhancement mechanism, where charge carrier separation enhancement at the photocatalyst surface is the dominant effect, whereas RhB adsorption does not make significant contribution. This significant insight enhances our understanding towards engineering highly efficient composite photocatalysts for organic pollutant removal in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic degradation
Black titanium dioxide
Graphene quantum dots
Surface adsorption
Charge separation
Water treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum Dot-Black TiO2 nanofiber composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: New Frontiers in Research Fund - Exploration

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Judy MacInnis (Cape Breton University) for assistance with the TEM. This work was supported by the Canada Research Chairs program, New Frontiers in Research Fund - Exploration (NFRFE-2018-01005), Nova Scotia Lands, Cape Breton University RISE program, and the NSERC Discovery Grants Program (RGPIN-2016-05569 and RGPIN-2015-03793).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Infrared spectra of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), black titanium dioxide nanofibers (bTiO2 NFs), and their 0.1–5.0 wt% composites.

Transmission electron microscopy images and selected area electron diffraction pattern (inset images) of (a) black titanium dioxide nanofibers, (b) graphene quantum dots, and (c) their 2.5 wt% composite.

Optical properties of graphene quantum dot-black titanium dioxide nanofiber (GQDs-bTiO2 NF) composites. (a) The UV–Vis and Tauc plots (inset) of the GQDs. (b) The normalized UV–Vis spectra of GQDs. (c) The fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 360 nm) of GQDs and their composites with bTiO2. (d) The GQDs photoluminescence spectra.
(a) Zeta potentials and (b) Rhodamine B (RhB) adsorption of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), black titanium dioxide (bTiO2), and their composites.
X-ray photoelectron spectra of (a, b, and c) black titanium dioxide (bTiO2) and (d, e, and f) 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2. (a and d) Ti 2p spectra, (b and e) O 1 s spectra, and (c and f) C 1 s spectra.

Photodegradation and photomineralization of Rhodamine B (RhB) by graphene quantum dots (GQDs), black titanium dioxide (bTiO2), and their composites. (a) Photodegradation of RhB by the composites and (b) their first-order kinetic plots. (c) Photomineralization of RhB by 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 NF composites after 180 min as determined by remaining total organic carbon (TOC, note the contribution of GQDs to the residual TOC); and (b) the photodegradation of RhB over four cycles.

Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra of (a) control experiments of 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP-OH) oxidation under varying conditions using TEMPO as a positive control and reactive oxygen species scavenging experiments for irradiated (b) black titanium dioxide (bTiO2), (c) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and (d) 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 composites.

Band structure and photocatalytic behaviour of black titanium dioxide (bTiO2) and 2.5 wt% GQD-bTiO2 nanofibers. (a) UV–Vis absorption and (inset) Tauc plots; (b) XPS valence band edge; and (c) energy diagram and photochemical reduction of molecular oxygen enhanced by GQD enabled charge separation.

head.ce:title: Mechanistic insight into the enhanced photodegradation by black titanium dioxide nanofiber-graphene quantum dot composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Cape Breton University, 1250 Grand Lake Road, Sydney, Nova Scotia B1P 6L2, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Cape Breton University, 1250 Grand Lake Road, Sydney, Nova Scotia B1P 6L2, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Damghan University, Damghan 3671641167, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Cape Breton University, 1250 Grand Lake Road, Sydney, Nova Scotia B1P 6L2, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biology, Cape Breton University, 1250 Grand Lake Road, Sydney, Nova Scotia B1P 6L2, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic activity of black titanium dioxide nanofibers (bTiO2 NFs) can be enhanced through composition with carbon nanomaterials. However, it is unclear whether enhanced charge separation or increased surface adsorption of organic pollutants to the nanocomposite surfaces is the dominant contributor. This knowledge gap was filled when we sought to enhance the surface contact of carbon with the bTiO2 NFs by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hypothesizing that this would further enhance photocatalytic activity. The results showed a similar Rhodamine B (RhB) photodegradation rate between the optimal loadings of GQDs and RGO we reported previously. This allowed for further elucidation of the enhancement mechanism, where charge carrier separation enhancement at the photocatalyst surface is the dominant effect, whereas RhB adsorption does not make significant contribution. This significant insight enhances our understanding towards engineering highly efficient composite photocatalysts for organic pollutant removal in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Black titanium dioxide (bTiO2) is a photocatalyst with a unique narrow bandgap originating from mid-bandgap traps that has been of interest since its discovery in 2011 [1]. TiO2 reduction through hydrogenation yields an amorphous layer composed of Ti4+/Ti3+ with oxygen vacancies that lower the bandgap energy and concurrently increase visible and NIR light absorption. The impurities are also inner lattice traps, which increase the exciton lifetime, subsequently increasing the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation rate and photocatalytic activity [1–3]. However, its photocatalytic activity is further enhanced through composition with carbon-based nanomaterials [4], including graphene oxide [5], reduced graphene oxide (RGO) [6–9], carbon nanotubes [10,11], g-C3N4 [12,13], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [14,15]. GQDs are exciting co-catalysts because of their optical properties, tunable size, and up/down-conversion properties [16], which significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 [17], ZnO [18], and CdS [19] under visible light irradiation. For example, the addition of 0.5 wt% of GQDs to g-C3N4 increased Rhodamine B (RhB) photodegradation by 42% via inhibition of charge carrier recombination [20]. Similarly, Chen et al. showed that the addition of only 0.4 wt% of nitrogen-doped GQDs to BiO2-x nanosheets improved their tetracycline photodegradation rate by 4.0, 2.9, and 5.5-fold under full-spectrum, visible, and near-infrared irradiation, respectively, relative to pure BiO2-x [21].

Development of a deep mechanistic understanding of the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composite materials is critical for engineering better composite materials. One important question is how carbon nanomaterials contribute to the enhanced photoactivity of the nanocomposite. Previously, we identified that surface adsorption of organic pollutant molecules to TiO2 surfaces was critical to determining the photodegradation efficiency and pathway [22]. Recently, our work on RGO-bTiO2 composites demonstrated significant high efficiency in ROS production and photodegradation of pollutant molecules [9]; however, the contribution made by RGO was unclear, i.e., whether through enhanced charge separation or increased surface adsorption of the pollutants. To address this question, we investigated the photocatalytic enhancement of GQDs-bTiO2 NF composites. The small GQDs attach to bTiO2 NFs with excellent surface contact. Results show a 2.7-fold increment in degradation compared to the bTiO2 NFs alone caused by forming a high-flux of superoxide radical anions that can degrade the dye at a higher rate. However, the first-order RhB photodegradation rate constants for optimal GQD-bTiO2 NF and RGO-bTiO2 NF materials are statistically similar (k = 0.0191 ± 0.0008 and 0.0150 ± 0.0016 min−1, respectively (p = 0.06)) and show that the efficiency of material overlap within the composites is not critical. Differences in RhB adsorption between GQD and RGO composites further eliminate the relative importance of surface adsorption, leaving charge carrier separation on the bTiO2 NF surfaces as the dominant enhancement mechanism. To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first investigation of the photocatalytic activity of black TiO2 NFs and GQDs composites. Furthermore, these results combined with our previous study on RGO-bTiO2 NFs provides a unique and rigorous comparison between two similar carbon-based co-catalysts. Our findings demonstrate that the photocatalytic activity enhancement of the GQD-bTiO2 NF composite is primarily dominated by charge carrier separation, with RhB adsorption having minimal effect. These results have significant implications for the development of highly efficient composite photocatalysts.

Rhodamine B (RhB), sodium azide, sodium borohydride, sodium nitrate, 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP-OH), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO), potassium bromide, and titanium (IV) oxide (anatase nanopowder) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Oakville, ON, Canada). Citric acid (anhydrous), ethanol (anhydrous), hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, and tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) were obtained from VWR International Ltd. (Mississauga, ON, Canada). 2,3-Bis(2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide sodium salt (XTT) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). All reagents were used as received without further purification.

A Barnstead Nanopure system (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to produce nanopure water (18.2 MΩ cm) daily. A Nicolet Impact 410 FTIR spectrometer was used to study the functional groups of the synthesized materials. An S-4800 transmission electron microscope (80 kV, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) detector was used to study the nanomaterial morphology. The zeta potentials of the nanomaterials were measured using a Nanosizer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). The light source was a 11,002 SunLite solar simulator light (SSL) equipped with a xenon arc lamp and a < 420 nm cut-off filter (Abet Technologies, Milford, CT, USA). Photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a Silver-Nova Super Range TE Cooled spectrometer (StellarNet, Tampa, FL, USA) with 390, 470, and 502 nm LEDs (10 mW) and an MPL-F-266 laser (266 nm, 20 mW, Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Tech. Co., Ltd., Changchun, Jilin, China) as excitation sources. A microplate reader (TECAN infinite M1000pro, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used for all spectroscopic and fluorescence measurements using UV transparent Greiner 96-well flat-bottom polystyrol microplates (Greiner, Kremsmünster, Austria) and Nunclon 96-well flat-bottom black polystyrol plates (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA). ROS identification was performed using an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (microESR, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA) and a MULTI N/C UV HS TOC analyzer (Analytikjena, Jena, Germany) was used to analyze the total organic carbon (TOC). A Kratos AXIS Supra spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα source (15 mA, 15 kV) was used to conduct X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. To calibrate the instrument's work function, a binding energy (BE) of 83.96 eV was obtained for the Au 4f7/2 line of metallic gold, and the spectrometer dispersion was adjusted to yield a BE of 932.62 eV for the Cu 2p3/2 line of metallic copper. The Kratos charge neutralizer system was used for all specimens. Survey scan analyses were performed using an analysis area of 300 µm × 700 µm and a pass energy of 160 eV and 20 eV for survey and high-resolution analyses, respectively. High-resolution spectra were corrected for charge to the main line of the carbon 1s spectrum (adventitious carbon), set to 284.8 eV. Analysis of the spectra was done with CasaXPS software (version 2.3.14).

Citric acid (5.04 g, 24 mmol) and sodium hydroxide (1.34 g, 72 mmol) were dissolved in 40.0 ml of water and stirred to obtain a clear solution, which was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting orange solution was diluted with two volumes of absolute ethanol and centrifuged for 5 min at 10,000 rpm. The resultant white powder was collected and dried overnight in a freeze drier at −20 °C and stored in the dark until use in further experiments [15].

Commercially available anatase nanopowder (2 g) was dispersed into sodium hydroxide solution (30 ml, 10 M) and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 24 h in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave [23]. The obtained powder was then separated via centrifugation (10 min, 10,000 rpm) and washed multiple times with 0.1 M HCl solution and water. The obtained TiO2 NFs were converted to bTiO2 NFs via a hydrogenation process by mixing with NaBH4 (TiO2 NFs:NaBH4, 1:4 w/w) using a mortar and pestle to obtain a fine powder that was transferred into a crucible and heated to 650 °C for 6 h under a N2 atmosphere. The obtained gray powder was then washed thoroughly with 0.1 M HCl solution to remove any unreacted particles and then rinsed several times with nanopure water until the supernatant reached neutral pH. The powder was then dried overnight in an oven at 80 °C [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum Dot-Black TiO2 nanofiber composites
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs (required amount depends on the desired loading to obtain a total mass of 500 mg; e.g., to obtain 2.5% GQDs/bTiO2 NFs requires 12.5 mg of GQDs and 487.5 mg of bTiO2 NFs) were dispersed into 10 ml of nanopure water while bTiO2 NFs were ultrasonically dispersed into 20 ml of ethanol for 30 min. The solutions were then mixed and heated to 60 °C for 2 h. The resulting powder was isolated via centrifugation and washed with ethanol and nanopure water several times. The final powder was dried in an 80 °C oven overnight and stored in a dark container.

Stock solutions of photocatalysts (10 g l−1, 1 wt%, 10 ml) and RhB (500 mg l−1, ∼1 × 10-3 M, 50 ml) were prepared and stored in the dark when not in use. Working solutions were prepared daily via ultrasonication for 30 min from stock solutions.

To study the degradation efficiency of the materials, 4.5 mg of GQDs-bTiO2 NFs (0.1 wt%) were mixed into 4.5 ml of RhB solution (50 mg l−1, ∼1 × 10-4 M) and stirred in the dark for 45 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. Afterward, 15 aliquots (300 µl each, including one photocatalyst-free blank and three not irradiated to correct for RhB adsorption on the photocatalyst) of this suspension were dispensed into the wells of a 96-well plate and irradiated by SSL (light source positioned 10 cm away from the solutions). The entire contents of selected wells were periodically removed over the 3 h reaction time and stored in the dark in microcentrifuge tubes. After centrifuging the tubes (8000 rpm, 5 min) the supernatants were transferred into clean tubes and then all the samples (including the blank and those for adsorption measurement) were diluted to 500 µl to compensate for water evaporation during the photodegradation experiments. Finally, a 100 µl aliquot of each sample was transferred into a 96-well plate and UV–Vis quantification was performed by measuring the absorbance in the range of 450–600 nm. For reusability tests, the used photocatalyst was carefully recovered by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) and repeated washing with EtOH and water, which continued until no RhB absorption was observed in the supernatant.

In a Petri dish, GQD-bTiO2 NFs (15 mg, 0.1 wt%) were suspended in 15 ml of RhB (50 mg l−1, ∼1 × 10-4 M) solution and irradiated with SSL positioned 10 cm away for 3 h. TOC analysis was performed on the centrifuged supernatant sample (8000 rpm, 5 min), which was diluted to 20 ml to compensate for any water lost to evaporation (maximum possible [RhB] = 37.5 mg l−1, 7.83 × 10-5 M).

To track the presence of any possible ROS in the system, the conversion of TEMP-OH to TEMPO-OH was measured which yields a 1:1:1 triplet in its EPR spectrum. The addition of specific ROS scavengers prevents conversion to TEMPO-OH and peak formation. Solutions of TEMP-OH (250 mM) and GQDs/bTiO2 NFs (0.1 wt%) were either irradiated by an SSL for 1 h or stored in the dark before EPR analysis. Samples were also irradiated after incubating with t-BuOH (200 mM), sodium azide (500 mM), or XTT (100 mM), which are hydroxyl radical, singlet oxygen, and superoxide radical anion scavengers, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to assess the morphology and composition of the materials. 0.2–5 µm long bTiO2 NFs were observed (Fig. 2 a), and their selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (inset) match published anatase nanocrystalline structures where diffuse rings indicate a polycrystalline material with some amorphous phases caused by Ti3+ defects [1,9]. Small (∼2–5 nm) spherical GQDs also exhibit diffuse rings reflecting their polycrystalline structure (Fig. 2b) [26]. The GQDs adhere to the bTiO2 NFs in the 2.5 wt% composite, where random small bright spots along with a diffuse ring indicate the polycrystalline nature of both components in the composite (Fig. 2c).

To understand the possible contributions of GQDs towards enhancing the photocatalytic activity of bTiO2 NFs, their optical properties were studied by UV–Vis, fluorescence, and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The GQD UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a) has peaks at 227 and 360 nm corresponding to π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively, in the graphene structures [27]. These transition energies and the band gap of the GQDs were calculated through the Tauc plot (3.19 eV, Fig. 3a inset), which was consistent with previously published results [28–30]. Composition between GQDs and bTiO2 NFs appeared to result in a simple addition of their UV–Vis absorbance (Fig. 3b) The GQD fluorescence emission spectrum (380–750 nm, Fig. 3c) during 360 nm excitation has a broad peak centered at 475 nm corresponding to 2 nm GQDs; however, the peak broadness suggests some larger-sized GQDs are present, consistent with TEM observation [31,32]. Fluorescence quenching indicated composition with bTiO2 NFs [33]. Photoluminescence at varying excitation wavelengths, i.e., 266, 390, 470, and 502 nm (Fig. 3d), was used to probe any up or down conversion properties of the GQDs. The emission spectra centered around 520 nm demonstrated down-conversion.

Photocatalyst surface charge significantly impacts the absorption rate of charged pollutants [34]. Thus, the ζ potentials of the materials were investigated, with bTiO2 NFs and GQDs having ζ potentials of −47.2 and −2.1 mV, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The composition of 2.5 wt% GQDs with bTiO2 NF did not significantly change their ζ potential (-45.6 mV, p = 0.21).

The internal structures of the bTiO2 NFs and their 2.5 wt% GQD composite were assessed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. 5 ). The Ti2p spectrum of bTiO2 has two peaks at 458.90 and 464.62 eV and two peaks at 457.37 and 460.54 eV, corresponding to the Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 peaks of Ti4+ and Ti3+, respectively, which is consistent with the reduction of TiO2 near particle surfaces, resulting in an amorphous layer with defect sites [35]. The Ti2p spectrum of the 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 NF composite also has these peaks, but shifted to 485.62, 464.34, 458.80, and 459.93 eV, which was attributed to the coordination of surface Ti to GQDs. The O1s spectrum of bTiO2 has three peaks at 530.21, 531.17, and 532.52 eV, corresponding to TiO2, TiOH (from hydrogen introduction), and Ti2O3, respectively [36]. In the O1s spectrum of 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 NF composite, these peaks were blue-shifted to 529.82, 530.98, and 531.26 eV, respectively, and two additional peaks appeared at 530.34 and 531.48 eV, corresponding to COC, COH, and CO, indicating TiO bond breaking and new TiC bond formation [37]. The C1s spectrum of bTiO2 primarily originated from the XPS grid and could be deconvoluted into peaks at 284.8, 286.4, 287.8, and 289.0 eV, corresponding to CC and CH, COC and COH, CO, and OCO. In the C1s spectrum of 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 NF composite, the same peaks were present, along with peaks at 283.42 and 290.02 eV, attributed to CC and π-π* transitions from the GQDs [38].

Adsorption is often the first step in photodegradation processes. It also contributes to the removal of pollutants from the bulk solution onto the surface and must be considered in any heterogeneous degradation process [22]. Before initiating photodegradation experiments, the test solutions were allowed to equilibrate with the photocatalyst surfaces, i.e., bTiO2 NFs, GQDs, and their composites, for 30 min in the dark to evaluate the dye adsorption. bTiO2 NFs and all its composites exhibited similar RhB adsorption capacity (∼27%, Fig. 4b), with GQDs having limited adsorption and 2.5 wt% GQDs on white anatase (wTiO2) having intermediate RhB adsorption. Therefore, RhB adsorption is dominated by the bTiO2 in the GQDs-bTiO2 NF composites, which we anticipate was enabled by an increase in surface hydrophobicity through hydrogenation of anatase [22]. Interestingly, GQDs had no effect on RhB adsorption in contrast with that observed for RGO [9].

After initial adsorption was completed in the dark, the solutions were irradiated with SSL and the residual RhB concentration was recorded as a function of time over 3 h (Fig. 6 a). RhB photodegradation was modeled using first-order kinetics (Fig. 6b). Pristine bTiO2 NFs photodegraded ∼ 76% of the RhB within 3 h (k = 0.0079 min−1), with the degradation rate increasing up to 2.5 wt% GQD loading (k = 0.0180 min−1) for an increased rate of 2.28 times. Further increase in GQD loading lowered the photodegradation rate (k = 0.0105 min−1 for 5.0 wt% GQD loading). The optimal catalyst, with 2.5 wt% GQD loading, was compared to its wTiO2 analog, whose photodegradation rate (k = 0.0012 min−1) was 15 times lower. The critical role of anatase surface hydrogenation in photodegradation efficiency is consistent with our prior work [9]. However, the effect of GQDs is less straightforward, and there appears to be a point where its enhancement effects are outweighed by the reduction in accessible bTiO2 surface area.

Beyond the photodegradation ability of the catalysts, which was exemplified by the photobleaching of RhB, their photomineralization ability, i.e., the ability to photo-oxidize pollutants to carbon dioxide and water, was similarly demonstrated via measuring the total organic carbon (TOC) before and after photodegradation experiments. Herein the contribution of the loaded GQDs to the TOC was corrected via blank experiments without added RhB. The optimal photocatalyst, with 2.5 wt% GQDs loading, mineralized 44% of the initial TOC, with the residual 70% consisting of ∼ 26% from residual GQDs and 74% from RhB degradation products (Fig. 6c). This is consistent with the UV–Vis data that showed that some residual degradation products remained after 3 h (data not shown). It is possible that some GQDs are lost through the generation of oxidizing species by the photocatalyst as they should be no less susceptible to oxidation than the pollutant molecules. When reusing the 2.5 wt% GQDs–bTiO2 there is a 5.9% drop in photodegradation activity during the third cycle (Fig. 6d) that can be attributed to the degradation of surface-bound GQDs.

The photodegradation and mineralization imparted by the GQDs-bTiO2 composites are mediated by the photocatalytic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The ROS generated in the system were determined using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy paired with selective scavenging agents. 4-Hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP-OH) was used as a non-selective probe for ROS generation through the formation of its N-oxyl radical (TEMPO-OH), which has a distinct 1:1:1 triplet in its EPR spectrum. The only scavenger that prevented TEMPO-OH radical formation was XTT, which selectively scavenges superoxide radical anions; t-butyl alcohol and sodium azide, which scavenge hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen, respectively, had no effect (Fig. 7 ). The Tauc plots from the UV–Vis absorption data were used to measure the bandgaps of the photocatalysts (Fig. 8 a) which were calculated to be 2.36 eV and 3.05 eV for bTiO2 alone and their composite with GQDs, respectively. The valence band edges of the catalysts were also determined using XPS (Fig. 8b). From these, an energy diagram for the 2.5 wt% GQDs-bTiO2 NF composite was prepared to describe the photocatalytic processes occurring on the surface (Fig. 8c). Superoxide radical anion formation ((E°(O2/O2 −) = − 0.16 V) is consistent with the positions of the valence and conduction bands and bandgap of GQDs-bTiO2 composites.

Graphene quantum dots could contribute to the photocatalyst function through several potential mechanisms. First, GQDs could contribute to light harvesting efficiency by expanding the UV–vis-NIR adsorption spectrum of the composite materials and transferring this photoenergy to the bTiO2 NFs; however, the GQDs most strongly absorb UV light in regions already well-covered by bTiO2 NF absorption and are unlikely to provide a general benefit through enhanced light adsorption. Second, GQDs can down-convert and focus light to a wavelength corresponding to the bTiO2 bandgap energy. This reasoning is specious for two reasons, bTiO2 can already absorb light at energies higher than its bandgap and GQDs emit light at wavelengths shorter than that corresponding to the bTiO2 bandgap energy (>700 nm) regardless. Third, GQDs can independently photocatalytically generate ROS, including superoxide radical anions [39,40]. However, this should be a simple additive effect and the GQD effect on photodegradation rate constant is non-linear and is actually detrimental at high loadings. While this contribution cannot be completely discounted, we think this effect is less significant than others. Fourth, GQDs, like RGO sheets, may contribute to enhanced adsorption of pollutant molecules, increasing their effective concentration near the source of ROS generation. However, adsorption studies showed that GQDs do not contribute significantly to RhB adsorption. Finally, GQDs, like most carbon-based nanomaterials, can enhance charge-carrier separation at their interface with photocatalysts. This appears to be the only reasonable remaining enhancement mechanism that is consistent with our experiments. Increasing GQD loading increases photocatalytic efficiency through enhanced charge-carrier separation; however, at higher loadings the competing effects of bTiO2 surface coverage and internal ROS scavenging by adsorbed GQDs outperform the enhancement effect and photocatalytic efficiency starts dropping. Additionally, Fig. 3c shows that the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs is reduced by ∼ 80% upon composite formation with bTiO2. This decrease in fluorescence can be attributed to exciton separation at the interface, as demonstrated previously for GQDs in different composite materials [20,41,42].

Herein we demonstrate the effect of GQD composition on bTiO2 NF surfaces towards enhanced photodegradation and mineralization of RhB as a model pollutant. The strategy was to provide more efficient overlap between the carbon material and the NFs relative to using RGO with the hypothesis that this would provide greater photoactivity. However, we found that optimal loadings of GQDs or RGO provided similar photodegradation rate constants under identical conditions. This, however, also allowed for the elucidation of the inconsequential effect of RhB adsorption as RGO increases its adsorption significantly whereas GQDs have no such effect. Thus, the apparent enhancement mechanism is via enhanced charge carrier separation at the bTiO2 surface. Both GQDs and RGO provide similar efficacy and generate the same radical, superoxide radical anion, and the choice of the composite should be determined by other operational parameters in particular use cases, e.g., RGO composites have additional mechanical advantages and a larger mass of sacrificial carbon that allows for more reuse cycles before performance drops.

Ali Shafiee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Andrew J. Carrier: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Collins Nganou: Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Muhammad Fahad Ehsan: Methodology, Validation, Data curation. Behzad Aibaghi: Supervision. Ken D. Oakes: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Judy MacInnis (Cape Breton University) for assistance with the TEM. This work was supported by the Canada Research Chairs program, New Frontiers in Research Fund - Exploration (NFRFE-2018-01005), Nova Scotia Lands, Cape Breton University RISE program, and the NSERC Discovery Grants Program (RGPIN-2016-05569 and RGPIN-2015-03793).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Increasing Solar Absorption for Photocatalysis with Black Hydrogenated Titanium Dioxide Nanocrystals
